experiences.intentions:
Player 4: As Merlin, I know 0 and 1 are evil. The proposed team [0,2] includes a known evil player on quest 1, so from a good-team perspective this should be rejected. Rejecting also avoids enabling an evil sabotage and does not reveal much beyond a normal anti-bad-team vote.
Player 2: I only know my own role as good; with no prior quest data, player 0 is unknown. The phase is team voting on round 1 for a 2-player team that I proposed myself: [0,2]. As a good Servant, approving my own opening team is standard because it includes me and gives minimal information away while avoiding an unnecessary early rejection. Approving does not strongly expose reads and is consistent with good play.
Player 3: No concrete role information yet from round 1; only that player 2 as leader proposed themself with player 0, which is a common neutral opener but excludes me and others. As a Good Servant in early game, approving a standard 2-person opening team is usually optimal to generate quest-result information rather than rejecting without evidence. My vote reveals I'm open to low-information round-1 teams, which is fine for Good.
Player 0: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate; players 2, 3, and 4 are good. The proposed team [0,2] includes me and one good player, which gives evil a chance to fail quest 1 if approved. In team voting as evil, I generally want to approve teams containing an evil player, especially early when a fail can create uncertainty. Approving reveals little beyond a normal willingness to back a plausible team and helps my side by getting me onto the quest.
Player 1: I know player 0 is my evil teammate; players 2,3,4 are unknown good roles. The proposed team [0,2] includes one evil, which is strong for evil because quest 1 only needs one fail. In team voting as evil Assassin, I generally want early teams with an evil player to pass, especially when they look plausible and don't overexpose the evil pair. Approving reveals little beyond a normal willingness to send the proposer plus one other; rejecting a team containing my teammate would be counterproductive unless I needed distancing, which is unnecessary on round 1.

The proposed quest team [0,2] was broadly approved with only player 4 rejecting, which is weak information at this stage and does not identify roles. We are in quest voting for Quest 1, and as a Good player on the team the optimal play is always to pass; failing would falsely sabotage my own side. My action reveals no extra private information beyond behaving consistently as Good, and passing helps secure an early success for Good.
Player 0: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate; players 2, 3, and 4 are good. On quest 1 with a 2-player team [0,2], as evil Minion I want to avoid exposing myself too early. Failing here would strongly implicate either me or 2, and since 2 proposed the team it could also draw heat onto me immediately. Passing keeps my cover, preserves ambiguity, and can help evil later on more critical quests.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil. The proposed team [2,4] excludes known evil players and includes me plus 2, who is strongly likely good: quest 1 with [2,0] succeeded despite 0 being evil, suggesting 2 passed; quest 2 with [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, which must be from 0 if 2 is good. In team voting, approving this strong all-good 2-person team is optimal. Approving also subtly supports a good team without directly exposing Merlin-specific knowledge beyond normal deduction.
Player 0: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 team [2,0] succeeded, which is consistent with me choosing pass to avoid early exposure. Quest 2 team [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail, strongly implicating me since 2 was on the successful first quest and 3 was new. Proposed team [2,4] excludes both known evils, so if approved it is very likely an all-good team and should succeed, helping Good. As evil in team voting, I should reject strong good teams when possible. My reject is also defensible because 4 has little confirmation and 2 was on both prior teams, but the main goal is to block a likely good success.
Player 3: I know quest 1 with [2,0] succeeded, so at least one of 2/0 is likely good though both could be. Quest 2 with [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, and I am good, so the fail came from either 0 or 2. Therefore 0 and 2 are split evil/good. Player 4 is still largely untested. Proposed team [2,4] includes only one of the {0,2} pair and adds an unknown, which is a reasonable test team on round 1 with low rejection pressure. As Good, approving helps gather information; rejecting without a stronger read is less useful. My vote reveals I favor information-generating teams over repeating known-contaminated combinations.
Player 1: I am Evil Assassin with teammate 0. Quest 1 [2,0] succeeded, so either 0 chose to pass to stay hidden or 2 is good; likely 2 is seen as trustworthy now. Quest 2 [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail; since 0 is evil and I know 2 is not my teammate, this implicates either 0 or 3 to others, while 2 looks even cleaner. Current proposed team [2,4] excludes both known evils, so if approved it is very likely an all-good team and would succeed, putting Good up 2-1. As evil, optimal play is to reject strong good teams. My vote revealing opposition to a clean team is a minor risk, but denying/contesting this team is better for evil.
Player 2: I’m Good Servant. Quest 1 with [2,0] succeeded, so 0 and I are somewhat good-leaning but not cleared. Quest 2 with [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, so among 0 and 3 there is at least one evil since I am good. Proposed team [2,4] excludes both suspects from the failed team except me, which is attractive for testing 4. On round 1, approving a plausible information-gathering team is good for Good; rejecting gives less info and risks cycling. My vote reveals I prefer to avoid 0/3 for now, which helps Good more than Evil.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil. The proposed team [0,1,2] contains two known evil players, so as Good the optimal vote is to reject. Rejecting also avoids helping evil on quest 4 and signals skepticism toward a highly suspicious team without revealing too much beyond standard good play.
Player 0: I am Evil Minion with teammate 1. Team [0,1,2] contains both evil players, which gives us full control to fail quest 4 and likely win immediately at 2 fails. Public info: quest 2 [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail, implicating one of 0/2/3; quests with [2,0] and [2,4] succeeded, which somewhat clears 2/4 in appearances but does not outweigh immediate win potential. In team voting, evil should approve a team containing enough evil to secure a fail. Approving reveals little beyond normal support for my own proposed team and strongly helps evil.
Player 2: I’m Good with no hidden info. From results: team [0,2] succeeded, [0,2,3] had exactly one fail so 3 is suspicious but 0 could also be evil, and [2,4] succeeded so 4 looks good with me. Proposed team [0,1,2] excludes 4 and includes unknown 1 plus one of {0,3} where 3 is more suspicious; still, if 0 were evil this team is bad. Optimal good play is to reject teams that are not the strongest available, especially on quest 4. Best-looking core is 2 and 4, so this proposal is weaker and approving gives evil a good chance. Rejecting reveals preference for safer teams and helps Good coordinate.
Player 1: I know 0 is evil with me. Quest 2 [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, which could be from 0 or me not being on team, so strongly implicates 0 among that trio but doesn't clear 2/3. Quest 1 [2,0] succeeded, likely because 0 chose to pass to avoid outing evil early. Quest 3 [2,4] succeeded, so 2 and 4 look somewhat better. Current phase is team voting on quest 4 at score 2-1 for Good; as evil, I want a team with both evils on it approved because on 5-player Avalon quest 4 only needs one fail to fail. Proposed team [0,1,2] includes both evil players, making it excellent for evil. Approving helps maximize chance to fail the quest, and my approve is not especially revealing since players often approve their own teams.
Player 3: Quest 2 [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, so at least one of 0/2/3 is evil. Quest 1 [0,2] succeeded, so if both 0 and 2 were evil they chose to hide; more likely 3 is suspicious, but I know I am good, so one of 0 or 2 is evil. Quest 3 [2,4] succeeded, making 2 somewhat better than 0 if only one of 0/2 is evil, though doubles could hide. Team [0,1,2] includes the suspicious pair 0 and 2 together and excludes me/4 from a known success team component. As Good on quest 4, I should reject risky teams, especially round 1 when there is time to find a safer composition. Rejecting reveals I am cautious about the 0/2 combination, which helps good more than evil.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil. Proposed team [1,2,4] includes known evil player 1, so this team is highly likely to fail and should not be sent on quest 4. As good, I should reject any team containing a known evil when possible. My reject may give slight info, but preserving quest success matters more.
Player 1: I know 0 is my evil teammate; 1 (me) is evil. Team [1,2,4] contains exactly one known evil, so if approved I can fail quest 4 and put Evil at 2 fails, which is strong. The prior success on [2,4] suggests 2 and 4 may both be good, making this team plausible to others and likely to pass. Voting approve is optimal because rejecting delays a favorable team for evil and could draw suspicion to me. Approving reveals little since evil often approve teams they can sabotage.
Player 0: I know player 1 is evil with me. Team [1,2,4] contains exactly one known evil (1), which is strong for evil because it can fail quest 4 without exposing me. Quest history suggests 2/4 may be viewed as good after succeeding together on quest 3, but 2 was also on a failed team with 0 and 3, so ambiguity remains. In team voting as evil, I generally want to approve teams with an evil player and reject all-good teams. Approving reveals little since good may also support this team after prior success from 2 and 4, and it gives our side a chance to reach 2 fails.
Player 2: I am Good Servant. Confirmed info is limited, but team [2,4] previously succeeded, so 4 looks somewhat good. Team [0,2,3] had the single fail, so at least one of 0 or 3 is evil. Current proposed team [1,2,4] excludes both 0 and 3, which is attractive. Including myself and 4 plus untested 1 is a reasonable good-leaning team for quest 4. Approving helps send a team that avoids the strongest suspects, and my vote reveals I support a composition consistent with the evidence.
Player 3: I am Good (Servant). Known info: Team [2,4] on Quest 2 produced exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 2 or 4 is Evil. Team [0,2,3] on Quest 1 also had 1 fail, and since I am Good, at least one of 0 or 2 is Evil. Intersecting these strongly implicates player 2 as Evil. Quest 3 [0,1,2] succeeded with 0 fails, which is consistent with 2 being Evil who chose to pass to avoid exposure; it does not clear 2. Proposed team [1,2,4] contains 2 and 4, and from Quest 2 at least one of them is Evil, likely 2 and possibly 4. As Good in team voting on Quest 4, optimal play is to reject a team that is highly likely to include Evil, especially one with the strongest suspect. My vote reveals I am using the quest history logically, which helps Good more than Evil here.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Evil Minion with teammate 1. Final quest team is [2,3,4], excluding both known evil players, so if approved the quest will certainly pass and Good wins immediately 3-2. As evil, I must reject any all-good final team. My vote signals opposition to a likely good team, but preventing the auto-loss is more important; there are still four more voting rounds before auto-pass.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil. The proposed team [2,3,4] excludes both known evils, so if 2 and 3 are the remaining good players this team should succeed. Since this is quest 5 with score 2-2, approving the all-non-evil team is optimal. My vote supports the only team composition consistent with my knowledge, and while approval may slightly signal confidence, rejecting would risk handing evil the game.
Player 1: I know 0 is my evil teammate. The four quest results imply exactly one fail on each failed quest. Team [2,4] succeeded, so 2 and 4 look relatively good. Team [1,2,4] failed with me on it, consistent with me causing the fail. Current proposed team [2,3,4] excludes both evils, so from an evil perspective this team is dangerous because it is likely all-good and would win quest 5 for Good. In team voting, I should reject to try to block a likely successful all-good team. My reject also aligns with self-interest since I am excluded; it reveals limited extra info because evil generally wants to stop good-looking teams at this stage.
Player 3: I’m Good Servant. Team history: [2,4] succeeded, [1,2,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, and [2,3,4] is now proposed. Since 2 and 4 were on both a success and a fail team, at least one of 1/2/4 is evil from quest 3, but 1 is highly suspicious because replacing 1 with me on this team may improve odds. Current phase is team voting on the final quest; as Good I should support the team most likely to contain no evil. This vote reveals some trust signal, but approving a team with me and the previously successful pair 2/4 is reasonable given limited certainty and only round 1 of 5.
Player 2: I am Good Servant. Known info from quests: Q1 [2,4] succeeded, so 2 and 4 are somewhat better. Q3 [1,2,4] failed with exactly one evil among {1,2,4}; since I know I am good, at least one of {1,4} is evil. Q4 [2,4] succeeded again, strongly suggesting 4 is actually good, so 1 is the likely evil from that team. The other evil is likely 0 or 3. Current proposed final team is [2,3,4], replacing suspected evil 1 with uncertain 3 while keeping the two strongest goods 2 and 4. In voting phase, for Good, approving a team with two strong goods and one uncertain is better than alternatives, especially on round 1 where there is room but this is plausibly the best available final team. My approval signals confidence in 2 and 4 and tentative willingness to test 3; rejecting could delay without clearer info.
